title: Universality and Chaos in Two-Dimensional Classical Ising Spin Glasses
speaker: David Huse
affiliation: Princeton University; Member, School of Mathematics

proof_stages.label: Two-dimensional Edwards-Anderson setting and numerical regime
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the 2D Edwards-Anderson Ising spin glass, the Gaussian and plus-minus-J coupling distributions, the absence of a nonzero-temperature spin-glass phase in 2D, and the low-temperature finite-size regime accessible by planar-graph algorithms and large numerics.

proof_stages.label: Gaussian zero-temperature droplet stiffness
proof_stages.summary: For continuous couplings the finite system has only the global spin-flip ground-state degeneracy, and low-lying large-scale excitations are optimized droplet/domain-wall excitations whose energy scale is L^theta with theta < 0 and fractal wall dimension about 1.3, measured via periodic/antiperiodic boundary-condition domain walls.

proof_stages.label: Finite-temperature entropy and temperature chaos in the Gaussian model
proof_stages.summary: At positive low temperature, active small droplets near a large droplet wall produce entropy fluctuations of order T^{1/2}L^{d_f/2}, so the free energy Delta F = Delta E - T Delta S crosses from energy-dominated behavior to an entropy-dominated chaotic regime at a crossover length L_x(T).

proof_stages.label: Gaussian correlation lengths and observable chaos
proof_stages.summary: The spin-glass correlation length is obtained by setting the typical droplet free energy equal to T, giving xi(T) ~ T^{1/theta}, while two-temperature correlations decay at the separate chaos/crossover scale L_x(T), and the numerics observe this through temperature-dependent free-energy crossings of boundary-condition domain walls.

proof_stages.label: Zero-temperature structure of the plus-minus-J model
proof_stages.summary: For discrete plus-minus-J couplings, the ground state has extensive zero-temperature entropy, the domain-wall energy distribution remains discrete and order-one with theta_e = 0, and the periodic/antiperiodic entropy difference scales as L^{theta_s} with theta_s about 1/2.

proof_stages.label: Plus-minus-J crossover to the universal chaotic regime
proof_stages.summary: At finite temperature the random entropy term broadens the initially discrete plus-minus-J free-energy distribution until, beyond L_x(T) ~ T^{-1/theta_s}, it matches the Gaussian universal droplet regime with the same length exponent theta but different temperature prefactors, yielding xi(T) ~ T^{1/theta - 1/theta_s}.

proof_stages.label: Thermodynamic consequence and finite-size visibility
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that the Gaussian low-temperature specific heat is governed by smallest-scale active spins and is linear in T, whereas the plus-minus-J specific heat is governed by droplets at the crossover scale, producing a different power law that became visible only once simulations reached sizes above roughly 100 by 100.

examples.name: Three-dimensional Ising spin-glass contrast
examples.note: The 3D case, with an apparent nonzero-temperature spin-glass transition and small positive stiffness exponent, is used only to situate 2D relative to the lower critical dimension.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses three-dimensional Ising spin glasses only as a contrast, stating facts such as a nonzero-temperature transition and a likely positive small theta without working through the 3D case.

passes.reason: The 3D Edwards–Anderson Ising spin glass is used only as a comparison case with positive stiffness/nonzero transition, and is a typical nondegenerate dimension rather than a classification exception.
A three-dimensional Edwards–Anderson Ising spin glass is used only as an ordinary contrast with the two-dimensional case.
ordinary three-dimensional Edwards-Anderson Ising spin glass used only to contrast its nonzero-temperature transition with the two-dimensional case
ordinary 3D Edwards-Anderson Ising spin glass used only to contrast its finite-temperature transition with the 2D case
ordinary 3D Edwards-Anderson spin glass used only to contrast its nonzero-temperature transition with the 2D case
An ordinary 3D Edwards-Anderson Ising spin glass is used only as a contrast with the 2D zero-temperature transition case.
The three-dimensional Edwards--Anderson spin glass is used only as a dimensional contrast to the two-dimensional case, and is an ordinary non-sporadic model rather than a structural extreme.
Used only as a dimensional contrast to 2D behavior; a 3D Edwards–Anderson Ising spin glass is not structurally sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric.
An ordinary three-dimensional Edwards–Anderson Ising spin glass is invoked only to contrast its nonzero-temperature transition with the two-dimensional case.
Used only as a contrast to the two-dimensional zero-temperature case, and the 3D Edwards–Anderson Ising spin glass is a standard non-sporadic model.
ordinary 3D Edwards-Anderson spin glass used as a dimensional comparison with the 2D case, not as a refutation
Used only as a comparison to 2D, the 3D EA spin glass is the first integer-dimensional case on the finite-temperature ordered side of the lower-critical-dimension boundary.

examples.name: One-dimensional Gaussian spin-glass chain
examples.note: In response to a question about why the stiffness exponent is negative, the speaker notes that in 1D the droplet cost is set by the weakest bond in a length-L chain, giving theta = -1.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly derives the one-dimensional Gaussian chain stiffness scaling by arguing that a length-l chain is controlled by its weakest bond, giving theta = -1.

passes.reason: The 1D Gaussian spin-glass chain is a minimal, essentially trivial base case where the droplet energy is set by the weakest bond, used heuristically to explain negative stiffness.
The one-dimensional Gaussian spin-glass chain is a minimal base case with reduced structure, used heuristically to illustrate negative stiffness exponent.
minimal loopless one-dimensional spin-glass chain where the domain-wall energy reduces to the weakest bond, giving the boundary case theta = -1
minimal one-dimensional, essentially gauge-trivial spin-glass chain whose excitation scale is controlled by the weakest bond
minimal one-dimensional chain with no frustration loops, used as a base-case illustration that the stiffness exponent can be negative
The 1D Gaussian chain is the minimal-dimensional base case where a domain wall reduces to cutting the weakest bond, used illustratively.

The one-dimensional Gaussian spin-glass chain is invoked as a simple base case where the weakest bond gives theta=-1, making it structurally collapsed relative to higher-dimensional frustrated spin glasses.
Used as a simple explanatory low-dimensional case for negative stiffness exponent; the 1D chain is a minimal, essentially loopless spin-glass setting.
The one-dimensional Gaussian chain is a minimal, essentially loopless base case used heuristically to explain negative stiffness.
Used as a lowest-dimensional/base-case illustration where the excitation is governed by the weakest bond and gives the boundary value θ=-1.
minimal one-dimensional loopless spin-glass base case, used heuristically to illustrate a negative stiffness exponent
The 1D Gaussian spin-glass chain is a solvable endpoint/base case with no loop frustration, where the weakest bond controls the stiffness exponent.

examples.name: Directed polymer/KPZ analogy
examples.note: An audience question compares the spin-glass optimization exponents with KPZ/direct-polymer exponents; the speaker treats this as an analogy, noting similar glassy scaling and temperature chaos but no direct connection.
examples.engagement_reason: The directed polymer/KPZ case is invoked as an analogy with similar glassy scaling and known chaos behavior, but no calculation or construction is carried out on it.

passes.reason: The directed polymer/KPZ model is invoked as an analogy for glassy scaling and temperature chaos, not as a disproof, and is a standard member of its universality setting.
The directed-polymer/KPZ model is invoked as a related ordinary analogy for glassy scaling and temperature chaos, not to refute a claim.
standard directed-polymer/KPZ universality example invoked only as an analogy for glassy scaling and temperature chaos
standard directed-polymer/KPZ model invoked only as an analogy for glassy scaling and temperature chaos
standard directed-polymer/KPZ model cited only as an analogy and earlier setting for temperature chaos
The directed polymer/KPZ model is a standard random-media analogy for similar glassy scaling, not a refuting example.
The directed polymer/KPZ model is mentioned only as an analogy for glassy scaling and temperature chaos, not as an obstruction, and it is a standard member of its universality class.
Used as an analogy for glassy/KPZ scaling and temperature chaos, not to refute a claim; directed polymers in random media are a standard model rather than structurally exceptional.
The directed-polymer/KPZ model is a standard analogous glassy-scaling model, not a sporadic or boundary object and not used to refute a claim.
Invoked only as an analogy from directed polymers/KPZ scaling and temperature chaos, not as a structurally exceptional object or refutation.
standard directed-polymer/KPZ model cited only as an analogy for glassy scaling and temperature chaos
The directed-polymer/KPZ model is invoked only as an analogy for glassy scaling and temperature chaos, and is a standard member of its universality class.
